request.question: Activated charcoal and zeolites are both categorized by having pores equal to or smaller than what size?

title: Prussian blue (medical use)
Capillary fringe
Cigarette filter
Activated charcoal (medication)
Nikolay Zelinsky
Trichloroacetyl chloride
Pore (bread)
Nanoporous materials
Molecular sieve
Activated carbon
sentences: Prussian blue, also known as potassium ferric hexacyanoferrate, is used as a medication to treat thallium poisoning or radioactive cesium poisoning.
For thallium it may be used in addition to gastric lavage, activated charcoal, forced diuresis, and hemodialysis.
It is given by mouth or nasogastric tube.
Prussian blue is also used in the urine to test for G6PD deficiency.
The capillary fringe is the subsurface layer in which groundwater seeps up from a water table by capillary action to fill pores.
Pores at the base of the capillary fringe are filled with water due to tension saturation.
This saturated portion of the capillary fringe is less than total capillary rise because of the presence of a mix in pore size.
If pore size is small and relatively uniform, it is possible that soils can be completely saturated with water for several feet above the water table.
Alternately, the saturated portion will extend only a few inches above the water table when pore size is large.
Capillary action supports a vadose zone above the saturated base within which water content decreases with distance above the water table.
In soils with a wide range in pore size, the unsaturated zone can be several times thicker than the saturated zone.
A cigarette filter is a component of a cigarette, along with cigarette paper, capsules and adhesives.
The filter may be made from cellulose acetate fibre, paper or activated charcoal (either as a cavity filter or embedded into the cellulose acetate).
Macroporous phenol-formaldehyde resins and asbestos have also been used in cigarette filters.
The acetate and paper modify the particulate smoke phase by particle retention (filtration), and finely divided carbon modifies the gaseous phase (adsorption).

Filters can reduce "tar" and nicotine smoke yields up to 50%, with a greater removal rate for other classes of compounds (e.g., phenols), but are ineffective in filtering toxins such as carbon monoxide.
Most factory-made cigarettes are equipped with a filter; those who roll their own can buy them from a tobacconist.
Activated charcoal, also known as activated carbon, is a medication used to treat poisonings that occurred by mouth.
To be effective it must be used within a short time of the poisoning occurring, typically an hour.
It does not work for poisonings by cyanide, corrosive agents, iron, lithium, alcohols, or malathion.
It may be taken by mouth or given by a nasogastric tube.
Other uses include inside hemoperfusion machines.
Zelinsky studied at the University of Odessa and at the universities of Leipzig and Göttingen in Germany.
Zelinsky was one of the founders of theory on organic catalysis.
He is the inventor of the first effective filtering activated charcoal gas mask in the world (1915).
Trichloroacetyl chloride is the acyl chloride of trichloroacetic acid.
It can be formed by reacting chlorine with acetyl chloride or acetaldehyde in the presence of activated charcoal.
It is used in the manufacture of pharmaceuticals and plant protection compounds.
Pores are the air pockets found in leavened bread, where carbon dioxide from the fermentation process creates a network of primarily interconnected void structures.
The degree to which pores form are a major determiner in the texture ("crumb") of the bread.
Pore size varies between varieties of bread.
Sourdough bread is a variety with larger pores.
Rye bread has smaller pores and a denser crumb.
Nanoporous materials consist of a regular organic or inorganic framework supporting a regular, porous structure.
The size of the pores is generally 100 nanometers or smaller.
Most nanoporous materials can be classified as bulk materials or membranes.
Activated carbon and zeolites are two examples of bulk nanoporous materials, while cell membranes can be thought of as nanoporous membranes.
A molecular sieve is a material with pores (very small holes) of uniform size.
These pore diameters are similar in size to small molecules, and thus large molecules cannot enter or be absorbed, while smaller molecules can.

As a mixture of molecules migrate through the stationary bed of porous, semi-solid substance referred to as a sieve (or matrix), the components of highest molecular weight (which are unable to pass into the molecular pores) leave the bed first, followed by successively smaller molecules.
Some molecular sieves are used in chromatography, a separation technique that sorts molecules based on their size.
Other molecular sieves are used as desiccants (some examples include activated charcoal and silica gel).

Activated carbon, also called activated charcoal, is a form of carbon processed to have small, low-volume pores that increase the surface area available for adsorption or chemical reactions. "Activated" is sometimes substituted with "active".

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: Activated charcoal and zeolites are both categorized by having pores equal to or smaller than what size?
hop1_docs: Molecular sieve | A molecular sieve is a material with pores (very small holes) of uniform size.  These pore diameters are similar in size to small molecules, and thus large molecules cannot enter or be absorbed, while smaller molecules can.  As a mixture of molecules migrate through the stationary bed of porous, semi-solid substance referred to as a sieve (or matrix), the components of highest molecular weight (which are unable to pass into the molecular pores) leave the bed first, followed by successively smaller molecules.  Some molecular sieves are used in chromatography, a separation technique that sorts molecules based on their size.  Other molecular sieves are used as desiccants (some examples include activated charcoal and silica gel).

Mesoporous organosilica | Mesoporous organosilica (periodic mesoporous organosilicas, PMO) are a type of silica containing organic groups that give rise to mesoporosity.  They exhibit pore size ranging from 2 nm - 50 nm, depending on the organic substituents.  In contrast, zeolites exhibit pore sizes less than a nanometer.  PMOs have potential applications as catalysts, adsorbents, trapping agents, drug delivery agents, stationary phases in chromatography and chemical sensors.

Nanoporous materials | Nanoporous materials consist of a regular organic or inorganic framework supporting a regular, porous structure.  The size of the pores is generally 100 nanometers or smaller.  Most nanoporous materials can be classified as bulk materials or membranes.  Activated carbon and zeolites are two examples of bulk nanoporous materials, while cell membranes can be thought of as nanoporous membranes.

Activated carbon | Activated carbon, also called activated charcoal, is a form of carbon processed to have small, low-volume pores that increase the surface area available for adsorption or chemical reactions. "Activated" is sometimes substituted with "active".

Catamaran | A catamaran ( ) (informally, a "cat") is a multi-hulled watercraft featuring two parallel hulls of equal size.  It is a geometry-stabilized craft, deriving its stability from its wide beam, rather than from a ballasted keel as with a monohull sailboat.  Being ballast-free and therefore lighter than a monohull, catamarans often have a shallower draft (draught) than comparably-sized monohulls.  The two hulls combined also often have a smaller hydrodynamic resistance than comparable monohulls, requiring less propulsive power from either sails or motors.  The catamaran's wider stance on the water can reduce both heeling and wave-induced motion, as compared with a monohull, and can give reduced wakes.
Microporous material | A microporous material is a material containing pores with diameters less than 2 nm.  Examples of microporous materials include zeolites and metal-organic frameworks.

Hyaena | Hyaena is a genus comprising two of the living species of hyenas: the striped hyena ("Hyaena hyaena") from western Asia and northern Africa and the brown hyena ("Hyaena brunnea") from southern Africa.  The brown hyena has sometimes been placed in a separate genus "Parahyaena", or even included in the otherwise fossil genus "Pachycrocuta", but recent sources have tended to place it in "Hyaena".  The brown hyena's skull is larger than that of the striped hyena.  The male brown hyena is slightly larger than the female, while the sexes of the striped hyena are equally sized.  Both species are smaller than the spotted hyena, but larger than the aardwolf.  They are predominantly scavengers.
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found:
  - Activated charcoal / activated carbon:
    - "Activated carbon" page mentions the term "activated charcoal" (passage "Activated carbon" [4]) but gives no numeric cutoff.
    - "Nanoporous materials" states: "Activated carbon and zeolites are two examples of bulk nanoporous materials" (passage "Nanoporous materials" [3]).
    - "Nanoporous materials" also gives a size: "The size of the pores is generally 100 nanometers or smaller" (passage "Nanoporous materials" [3]).
  - Zeolites:
    - "Microporous material" lists zeolites as examples and defines microporous as "pores with diameters less than 2 nm" (passage "Microporous material" [6]).
    - "Mesoporous organosilica" notes "zeolites exhibit pore sizes less than a nanometer" (passage "Mesoporous organosilica" [2]).
- Ambiguities:
  - Two different classifications appear: "Nanoporous materials" (<=100 nm) vs "Microporous material" (<2 nm). Zeolites are referenced in both contexts (and also "<1 nanometer" in [2]); activated carbon is explicitly listed only under "Nanoporous materials" in the provided passages. The specific "Zeolite" page and detailed pore-size info for "Activated carbon" are not present in these passages.
- Bridge entity / Missing fact:
  - Bridge entity (as named in the passages): "Nanoporous materials" (also note "Microporous material" is relevant for zeolites).
  - Missing fact needed: the authoritative pore-diameter threshold (in nanometers) that classifies both activated charcoal and zeolites into the same pore-size category (i.e., which numeric cutoff—≤100 nm, <2 nm, <1 nm—applies to both).
- Retrieval direction (prioritized next hops):
  1) Open "Nanoporous materials" page — check lede/definition for the pore-size cutoff ("The size of the pores is generally...") and any notes listing activated carbon/zeolites as examples.
     - Search keywords: Nanoporous materials lede "100 nanometers or smaller" activated carbon zeolites.
  2) Open "Microporous material" page — check lede/definition and "Examples" for zeolites and the explicit "<2 nm" phrase.
     - Search keywords: Microporous material lede "<2 nm" zeolites examples.
  3) Open "Zeolite" page — check lede/infobox for typical pore-size (e.g., "<1 nm") and classification (micro/mesoporous/nanoporous).
     - Search keywords: Zeolite lede pore size "<1 nm" microporous.
  4) Open "Activated carbon" (Activated charcoal) page — check lede/infobox and sections on pore structure or "Properties" for its pore-size classification (micro/meso/macro) and numeric ranges.
     - Search keywords: Activated carbon pore size micropore mesopore macropore distribution.
Final answer deferred until those pages/sections are retrieved.
response.hop2_query: activated carbon zeolites pore size cutoff nanoporous microporous
hop2_docs: Nanoporous materials | Nanoporous materials consist of a regular organic or inorganic framework supporting a regular, porous structure.  The size of the pores is generally 100 nanometers or smaller.  Most nanoporous materials can be classified as bulk materials or membranes.  Activated carbon and zeolites are two examples of bulk nanoporous materials, while cell membranes can be thought of as nanoporous membranes.

Carbide-derived carbon | Carbide-derived carbon (CDC), also known as tunable nanoporous carbon, is the common term for carbon materials derived from carbide precursors, such as binary (e.g. SiC, TiC), or ternary carbides, also known as MAX phases (e.g., TiAlC, TiSiC).  CDCs have also been derived from polymer-derived ceramics such as Si-O-C or Ti-C, and carbonitrides, such as Si-N-C.  CDCs can occur in various structures, ranging from amorphous to crystalline carbon, from sp- to sp-bonded, and from highly porous to fully dense.  Among others, the following carbon structures have been derived from carbide precursors: micro- and mesoporous carbon, amorphous carbon, carbon nanotubes, onion-like carbon, nanocrystalline diamond, graphene, and graphite.  Among carbon materials, microporous CDCs exhibit some of the highest reported specific surface areas (up to more than 3000 m/g).  By varying the type of the precursor and the CDC synthesis conditions, microporous and mesoporous structures with controllable average pore size and pore size distributions can be produced.  Depending on the precursor and the synthesis conditions, the average pore size control can be applied at sub-Angstrom accuracy.  This ability to precisely tune the size and shapes of pores makes CDCs attractive for selective sorption and storage of liquids and gases (e.g., hydrogen, methane, CO) and the high electric conductivity and electrochemical stability allows these structures to be effectively implemented in electrical energy storage and capacitive water desalinization.
Microporous material | A microporous material is a material containing pores with diameters less than 2 nm.  Examples of microporous materials include zeolites and metal-organic frameworks.
Nanopore | A nanopore is a pore of nanometer size.  It may, for example, be created by a pore-forming protein or as a hole in synthetic materials such as silicon or graphene.
Nanopore battery | A nanopore battery is a rechargeable battery that is a composite of billions of nanoscale batteries formed within the pores of a substrate.

Mesoporous organosilica | Mesoporous organosilica (periodic mesoporous organosilicas, PMO) are a type of silica containing organic groups that give rise to mesoporosity.  They exhibit pore size ranging from 2 nm - 50 nm, depending on the organic substituents.  In contrast, zeolites exhibit pore sizes less than a nanometer.  PMOs have potential applications as catalysts, adsorbents, trapping agents, drug delivery agents, stationary phases in chromatography and chemical sensors.

Zeolite | Zeolites are microporous, aluminosilicate minerals commonly used as commercial adsorbents and catalysts.  The term "zeolite" was originally coined in 1756 by Swedish mineralogist Axel Fredrik Cronstedt, who observed that rapidly heating the material, believed to have been stilbite, produced large amounts of steam from water that had been adsorbed by the material.  Based on this, he called the material "zeolite", from the Greek ζέω ("zéō") , meaning "to boil" and λίθος ("líthos") , meaning "stone".  The classic reference for the field has been Zeolite molecular sieves: structure, chemistry, and use by Donald W. Breck.
response.summary_2: The size of the pores is generally 100 nanometers or smaller. Activated carbon is listed as an example of bulk nanoporous materials. Zeolites are microporous aluminosilicate minerals with pore diameters less than 2 nm.
